serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Turkish red lentil soup is approximately 300 g (one bowl), as typically served at Turkish restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Lentil soup, home-prepared — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB No. 06016)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for home-prepared lentil soup used as the closest approved match. Turkish mercimek çorbası is a red lentil soup with similar macronutrient profile; values adapted to reflect typical Turkish preparation with olive oil, tomato paste, cumin, and paprika. No Singapore-specific or Turkish-specific entry found in HPB Foodo or FAO/INFOODS SEA. USDA values are consistent with published Turkish dietary literature.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 44 is based on lentil soup (red lentils, boiled) from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). Turkish mercimek çorbası is a blended red lentil soup; blending may slightly raise GI compared to whole lentils (GI ~21–30), but published lentil soup values cluster around 44. GL per serving calculated as (44 × 7.6 g carbohydrate per 100g × 300g serving) / 100 = 10.0 g.

hypertension.guidance: Turkish red lentil soup contains approximately 280 mg sodium per 100 g (840 mg per 300 g bowl), which represents a significant portion of the 1500–2000 mg daily sodium target for hypertensive individuals; patients should request low-sodium preparation or limit portion size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and high dietary fibre
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a glycemic index of 44 (low) and 1.8 g dietary fibre per 100 g, Turkish red lentil soup produces a moderate glycemic load of approximately 10 g per 300 g serving, making it a suitable choice for blood glucose management in individuals with Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, high fibre, and plant-based protein
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Turkish red lentil soup is low in saturated fat (0.2 g per 100 g), contains no cholesterol, and provides plant-based protein and soluble fibre that support LDL cholesterol reduction, making it a heart-healthy option when prepared with minimal added salt.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dal, in the Turkish culinary context served at Sultan Palace restaurant in Singapore, refers to mercimek çorbası — a traditional Turkish red lentil soup. It is prepared by simmering red lentils with onion, carrot, and tomato paste in vegetable or chicken stock, then blending the mixture to a smooth, velvety consistency. The soup is finished with a spiced butter or olive oil tempering of cumin and paprika, which gives it a warm, earthy, and mildly smoky flavour. The texture is thick and creamy without the use of dairy. Nutritionally, it provides approximately 52 kcal per 100 g, with 2.8 g protein, 7.6 g carbohydrate, and 1.8 g dietary fibre. Its low glycemic index of 44 and high fibre content make it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or cardiovascular risk. The sodium content (~280 mg per 100 g) warrants attention for hypertensive individuals. Red lentils are a good source of folate, iron, and plant-based protein. This dish is naturally vegetarian, vegan, gluten-free, and halal. It is commonly served as a starter or light meal in Turkish and Middle Eastern restaurants across Singapore.
common_user_queries: Is mercimek corbasi good for diabetics?
How many calories in Turkish lentil soup?

What is the GI of lentil soup?
What does mercimek corbasi taste like?

confidence_reason: No exact Turkish mercimek çorbası entry was found in HPB Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Nutrient values are adapted from the USDA SR Legacy lentil soup entry (NDB 06016), which is the closest approved source. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database lentil soup reference. Confidence is medium due to the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: 1. The input record classifies this dish under 'Meat and alternatives' food group, which is retained as lentils are a primary protein source in this food group classification. 2. The restaurant context (Sultan Palace, Boat Quay) confirms this is a Turkish-style preparation. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero as red lentil soup contains no hydrogenated fats; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central source. 4. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients in the standard vegan preparation. 5. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant-based dish; red lentils contain no vitamin B12. 6. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as red lentil soup contains no vitamin D-rich ingredients; confirmed zero per USDA source. 7. monounsaturated_fat_g (0.9), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.2), potassium_mg (180.0), calcium_mg (18.0), iron_mg (1.1), vitamin_c_mg (2.0), vitamin_a_mcg_rae (28.0), vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg (0.08), vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg (0.04), vitamin_b3_niacin_mg (0.6), vitamin_b6_mg (0.1), folate_mcg_dfe (36.0), vitamin_e_mg (0.5), phosphorus_mg (68.0), magnesium_mg (16.0), zinc_mg (0.5), selenium_mcg (2.0), water_g (86.0) are all adapted from USDA SR Legacy lentil soup entry. 8. No -1 sentinel values remain unexplained; all micronutrients are populated from the USDA source. 9. GI of 44 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database lentil soup reference; Turkish blended preparation may have slightly higher GI than whole lentils but published soup values support this estimate.
